questions.prompt: When should you use Edge Functions instead of the auto-API?

choices.text: When logic doesn't fit a single SQL query or simple CRUD: webhooks from external services (Stripe, Twilio), calling third-party APIs that require secret keys (OpenAI, payment gateways), background processing of uploaded files, complex multi-step workflows, AI/LLM streaming endpoints, cron-triggered tasks (paired with pg_cron), and email sending with custom logic. The auto-API handles ~80% of operations as CRUD; Edge Functions handle the rest. Keep CRUD in the auto-API for free; reserve Edge Functions for genuine logic.

questions.explanation: Webhooks, external APIs with secrets, complex workflows, AI streaming, cron tasks.

questions.prompt: Edge Function that should act as the calling user (respecting RLS). How?

choices.text: Forward the Authorization header from the incoming request when creating the Supabase client inside the function: `createClient(url, anonKey, { global: { headers: { Authorization: req.headers.get('Authorization')! } } })`. The supabase client now carries the user's JWT; any queries through it run as the authenticated user with RLS applied. Call `supabase.auth.getUser()` to verify and retrieve the user. Use service_role only when explicitly bypassing RLS for admin operations after verifying the caller is authorized.

questions.explanation: Forward Authorization header to the Supabase client; RLS applies as that user.
